intent: Defensive identity, authentication, authorization, federation, and access-governance knowledge for secure architecture, operations, and policy reasoning.

principles.name: Identity Is a Core Control Plane
principles.summary: In cloud and hybrid environments, identity and session control often define trust boundaries more than network location.
notes: Treat identities, devices, sessions, and service principals as first-class security objects.

principles.name: Phishing-Resistant Authentication
principles.summary: Prefer authentication methods that are resistant to credential phishing and relay attacks over weaker factors like SMS.
coreIdeas: Use MFA broadly, prioritizing phishing-resistant methods where feasible.
Reduce reliance on shared secrets and weak recovery flows.
Treat enrollment, recovery, and reset paths as high-risk surfaces.

principles.name: Least Privilege, Separation of Duties, and JIT Access
principles.summary: Users, administrators, and workloads should receive the minimum access necessary, ideally only for the time needed.
coreIdeas: Use separation of duties for sensitive workflows.
Prefer just-in-time elevation and approval-based privileged access.

principles.name: Identity Lifecycle Governance
principles.summary: Provisioning, role change, leave of absence, contractor expiry, and offboarding must be accurate and timely to prevent access drift.
coreIdeas: Continuously review dormant, orphaned, and overprivileged accounts.
riskPatterns: Role changes that preserve excessive permissions.

principles.name: Continuous Verification
principles.summary: Authentication is not a one-time event; sensitive access decisions should consider session risk, device posture, context, and behavior.

controlFamilies.name: Authentication Assurance
controlFamilies.description: Controls for identity proofing, authenticator selection, enrollment, lifecycle, and recovery.

controlFamilies.name: Authorization & Entitlement Governance
controlFamilies.description: Role design, approval logic, periodic certification, and resource scoping to keep access rights bounded.

controlFamilies.name: Privileged Access Management
controlFamilies.description: Protect elevated accounts, administrative workflows, and privileged sessions.

controlFamilies.name: Machine Identity & Secrets Hygiene
controlFamilies.description: Protect service accounts, API keys, certificates, tokens, and workload identities.

controlFamilies.name: Identity Monitoring & Detection
controlFamilies.description: Monitor anomalous access, impossible travel, privilege abuse, token misuse, and risky sign-in behavior.

architecturalModels.name: Centralized Identity Provider Model
architecturalModels.summary: A primary IdP provides authentication and federation for workforce and business applications.
watchouts: misconfiguration can have broad blast radius

architecturalModels.name: Zero Trust Access Model
architecturalModels.summary: Access decisions consider user, device, workload, session, and resource context continuously, not just network location.

architecturalModels.name: Federation Model
architecturalModels.summary: Use federation for partner, customer, and workforce scenarios where trust is delegated across domains.

riskPatterns.description: Credential theft or session hijacking through phishing, adversary-in-the-middle pages, or token replay.
riskPatterns.impact: Account takeover, business email compromise, lateral movement, data access.

riskPatterns.description: Accumulated entitlements from role changes, exceptions, or temporary access that never expires.
riskPatterns.impact: Excessive access, audit failures, insider risk amplification.

riskPatterns.description: Improper federation trust or claim mapping grants unintended access to resources.
Secrets, certificates, or service tokens are embedded, shared, or poorly rotated.
riskPatterns.impact: Automated abuse, persistence, CI/CD compromise, cloud control-plane access.

riskPatterns.description: Weak password reset, help desk override, or account recovery flows become the easiest path into an account.
riskPatterns.impact: Bypass of strong authentication and durable account compromise.

privacy: Limit access to personal data by role and purpose.
Treat identity proofing, audit logs, and user lifecycle data as privacy-relevant records.
law: Access governance can affect breach reporting, evidentiary logging, and regulatory accountability.
finance: Segregation of duties, approval workflows, and transaction authorization reduce fraud risk.
operations: HR, ITSM, and vendor management data feeds are critical to clean lifecycle automation.

keywords: identity is a core control plane
least privilege, separation of duties, and jit access

nistDigitalIdentity: Use NIST SP 800-63-4 / 63B concepts for assurance levels, authenticator lifecycle, and recovery design.
Treat enrollment, recovery, and federation as equal citizens, not just the login screen.
zeroTrust: Map access decisions to identity, device, session, and resource context using NIST SP 800-207 principles.
Avoid implicit trust based only on network placement.
secureByDesign: Default toward safer authentication, safer admin boundaries, and smaller permission surfaces.

learningModules.title: Identity Fundamentals & Assurance
outcomes: Explain why weak recovery breaks strong MFA.
Distinguish authentication from authorization and federation.

learningModules.title: Authorization, Entitlements, and Access Reviews
outcomes: Identify privilege creep and toxic access combinations.

learningModules.title: Privileged Access Management
outcomes: Define guardrails for emergency admin access.

learningModules.title: SSO, Federation, and External Trust
outcomes: Explain how upstream identity quality affects downstream risk.

learningModules.title: Machine Identity, Secrets, and Workload Access
outcomes: Replace hardcoded credentials with managed approaches.
Build a review process for non-human identities.

learningModules.title: Identity Threat Detection & Response
outcomes: Translate suspicious login signals into containment actions.

recommendedProjects.name: Quarterly Access Review Matrix
recommendedProjects.description: Build a role-and-entitlement review workbook for privileged, finance, HR, and vendor accounts.

recommendedProjects.name: Break-Glass Account Governance Pattern
recommendedProjects.description: Define emergency account controls, approvals, storage, monitoring, and post-use review.

recommendedProjects.name: Service Account & Secret Inventory
recommendedProjects.description: Catalog machine identities, owners, scopes, storage methods, rotation dates, and unused credentials.

recommendedProjects.name: Federation Trust Map
recommendedProjects.description: Document IdP relationships, relying parties, claims, lifecycle dependencies, and trust assumptions.

caseStudies.title: Strong MFA, Weak Recovery
caseStudies.scenario: An organization rolled out MFA but left help-desk recovery with weak caller verification and broad override authority.
lessons: Recovery flows can become the soft underbelly of identity security.
Help-desk processes need scripts, escalation thresholds, and logging.

caseStudies.title: Session Token Theft on Admin Workstation
caseStudies.scenario: An attacker captures a high-value session token from an unmanaged or compromised endpoint and reuses it to access cloud administration resources.
lessons: Identity, device posture, and session protection must work together.
Admin access should be constrained to managed devices and bounded sessions.

caseStudies.title: Privilege Creep After Organizational Change
caseStudies.scenario: Several employees changed teams over six months and retained old entitlements because access removals were manual and incomplete.
lessons: Periodic certification and exception expiry prevent access drift.

caseStudies.title: Vendor Federation Claim Mapping Error
caseStudies.scenario: A partner federation relationship mapped a broad group claim into a high-access application role.
lessons: Federation should be tested like code.
Least-privilege defaults and explicit claim mapping reduce blast radius.

primary.title: Canadian Centre for Cyber Security ICAM guidance

secondary.title: Vendor architecture docs for IdP/PAM platforms
Peer-reviewed IAM and usable security literature
Textbooks and professional handbooks on IAM, cloud security, and enterprise architecture

tertiary.title: Security awareness explainers and orientation summaries

governanceNotes: Operate in defensive-only mode; do not generate offensive, intrusive, or exploit-enabling guidance.
Normalize retrieval through packId, packType, aliases, and aggregated retrievalIndex.
Source metadata is present, but direct URLs/DOIs/ISBNs are not consistently attached.

requiredShapeForDefinition: one practical safe control or clarification if needed

ambiguityPolicy.knownOverlapBehavior: Do not fail closed into generic fallback. Provide a brief neutral definition, name the possible domain angles, then ask one targeted clarification question.

crossDomainPairs.cyber_law: Ask whether the user means security controls/risk reduction or legal/compliance obligations.
crossDomainPairs.cyber_finance: Ask whether the user means security risk, fraud/financial exposure, or business impact.
crossDomainPairs.cyber_ai: Ask whether the user means security architecture, AI system safety, prompt injection, or model/tool behavior.
crossDomainPairs.cyber_psychology: Ask whether the user means human-risk/security culture or psychological behavior.
crossDomainPairs.cyber_english: Ask whether the user wants the cybersecurity definition or the plain-language wording/usage meaning.

runtimeDefinitionIndex.definition: The identity control plane is the layer where users, services, sessions, and permissions define who can access what and under what conditions.
Phishing-resistant authentication uses methods such as passkeys or hardware-backed authenticators that resist credential theft and relay attacks better than passwords or SMS codes.
Multi-factor authentication requires more than one proof of identity, reducing the chance that a stolen password alone can compromise an account.
Role-based access control assigns permissions through defined roles so access is easier to review, limit, and govern.
Attribute-based access control makes authorization decisions using attributes such as user role, device posture, resource sensitivity, location, or session risk.
Just-in-time access grants elevated privileges only when needed and usually for a limited time, reducing standing privilege risk.
Privileged access management protects administrator accounts and high-risk actions through vaulting, approvals, monitoring, and session controls.
Machine identity covers service accounts, API keys, certificates, tokens, and workload identities used by software rather than humans.
Access drift happens when permissions accumulate over time beyond what a person or service currently needs.
